coredata.dc:title: A fluorometric assay for alkaline phosphatase activity based on β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon quantum dots through host-guest recognition
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Cong
Qian, Zhaosheng
Huang, Yuanyuan
Xu, Jiamin
Ao, Hang
Zhao, Meizhi
Zhou, Jin
Chen, Jianrong
Feng, Hui
coredata.dc:description: A convenient, reliable and highly sensitive assay for alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity in the real-time manner is developed based on β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon quantum dots (β-CD-CQDs) nanoprobe through specific host-guest recognition. Carbon quantum dots were first functionalized with 3-aminophenyl boronic acid to produce boronic acid-functionalized CQDs, and then further modified with hydropropyl β-cyclodextrins (β-CD) through B-O bonds to form β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe. p-Nitrophenol phosphate disodium salt is used as the substrate of ALP, and can hydrolyze to p-nitrophenol under the catalysis of ALP. The resulting p-nitrophenol can enter the cavity of β-CD moiety in the nanoprobe due to their specific host-guest recognition, where photoinduced electron transfer process between p-nitrophenol and CQDs takes place to efficiently quench the fluorescence of the probe. The correlation between quenched fluorescence and ALP level can be used to establish quantitative evaluation of ALP activity in a broad range from 3.4 to 100.0U/L with the detection limit of 0.9U/L. This assay shows a high sensitivity to ALP even in the presence of a very high concentration of glucose. This study demonstrates a good electron donor/acceptor pair, which can be used to design general detection strategy through PET process, and also broadens the application of host-guest recognition for enzymes detection in clinical practice.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity
Real-time fluorometric assay
Photoinduced electron transfer (PET)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon quantum dots (β-CD-CQDs) probe
Fluorescence quenching of β-CD-CQDs by NP
Selectivity test of the ALP assay
Principle of ALP detection based on β-CD-CQDs
Synthesis and characterization of β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe
Photoinduced electron transfer process between β-CD-CQDs and p-nitrophenol
Real-time fluorescence assay for ALP activity

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are grateful to the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21405142 and 21275131 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of β-CD-CQDs (A) and (B) XPS spectrum of β-CD-CQDs. 1B inset: HRTEM image of β-CD-CQDs.
Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CQDs and β-CD-CQDs (A), and time-resolved decay curves of CQDs and β-CD-CQDs (B).

The change of fluorescence spectra of β-CD-CQDs in the presence of different concentration of p-nitrophenol (A), and time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of β-CD-CQDs in the absence and presence of p-nitrophenol (70.0µM) (B).

The change of fluorescence spectra of β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe in the presence of different composition. (a) mere β-CD-CQDs; (b) β-CD-CQDs, and NPP (70.0µM); (c) β-CD-CQDs, and p-nitrophenol (70.0µM); (d) β-CD-CQDs, NPP (70.0µM) and ALP (200.0U/L).

(A) The fluorescence quenching efficiency versus incubation time at different of different ALP levels (100, 200, 300U/L) in the mixture containing β-CD-CQDs and NPP (70.0µM). (B) Emission spectra of the mixture containing β-CD-CQDs and NPP (70.0µM) at varied ALP levels from 0.0 to 200.0U/L. (C) The fluorescence intensity of β-CD-CQDs versus ALP activity. (D) The fitting curve between quenched fluorescence and ALP activity in the range from 3.1 to 80.0U/L.
Schematic illustration of detection strategy for ALP activity based on β-CD-CQDs probe through host-guest recognition.

head.ce:title: A fluorometric assay for alkaline phosphatase activity based on β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon quantum dots through host-guest recognition

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University, Jinhua 321004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing National laboratory for Molecular Sciences, Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Living Biosystems, Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing National laboratory for Molecular Sciences, Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Living Biosystems, Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A convenient, reliable and highly sensitive assay for alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity in the real-time manner is developed based on β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon quantum dots (β-CD-CQDs) nanoprobe through specific host-guest recognition. Carbon quantum dots were first functionalized with 3-aminophenyl boronic acid to produce boronic acid-functionalized CQDs, and then further modified with hydropropyl β-cyclodextrins (β-CD) through B-O bonds to form β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe. p-Nitrophenol phosphate disodium salt is used as the substrate of ALP, and can hydrolyze to p-nitrophenol under the catalysis of ALP. The resulting p-nitrophenol can enter the cavity of β-CD moiety in the nanoprobe due to their specific host-guest recognition, where photoinduced electron transfer process between p-nitrophenol and CQDs takes place to efficiently quench the fluorescence of the probe. The correlation between quenched fluorescence and ALP level can be used to establish quantitative evaluation of ALP activity in a broad range from 3.4 to 100.0U/L with the detection limit of 0.9U/L. This assay shows a high sensitivity to ALP even in the presence of a very high concentration of glucose. This study demonstrates a good electron donor/acceptor pair, which can be used to design general detection strategy through PET process, and also broadens the application of host-guest recognition for enzymes detection in clinical practice.

ce:section.ce:para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP, EC 3.1.3.1), widely present in the tissues of living organisms, is a hydrolase enzyme which is able to catalyze the dephosphorylation process of a variety of phosphorylated substrates (Fernley, 1971). It has been proven that alkaline phosphatase plays a critical role in signal transduction and regulation of intracellular processes in cell growth, apoptosis and signal transduction pathways (Cohen, 2000; Johnsonand and Lewis, 2001; Julien et al., 2011; Yaffe, 2002). ALP in serum is frequently used as a important diagnostic indicator for various diseases including breast and prostatic cancer (Lorente et al., 1999), bone disease (Colornbatto et al., 1996a), liver dysfunction and diabetes (Colornbatto et al., 1996b). Therefore, it is of great significance in better understanding the role of ALP in related biological processes and clinical practice to develop reliable, convenient and sensitive assay methods for ALP level.

Colorimetric approach is an early general detection method for ALP activity because of its convenience (Baykov et al., 1988; Harbron et al., 1991). A further development on colorimetic detection of ALP based on different silver or gold nanoparticles were realized (Gao et al., 2014; Jiao et al., 2014; Li et al., 2013; Wei et al., 2008), but fluorometric approach shows more desirable performance in comparison with colorimetry because of its high sensitivity and real-time monitoring capability. Up to date, a variety of fluorescent assays for ALP have been reported using organic dyes (Gong et al., 2011; Gu et al., 2013; Park et al., 2014; Song et al., 2014), fluorescent polymers (Deng et al., 2015; Li et al., 2014; Liu and Schanze, 2008; Zeng et al., 2012; Zheng et al., 2014), inorganic semiconductor dots (QDs) or metal nanoclusters (Freeman et al., 2010; Jiao et al., 2013; Liu et al., 2014) as fluorescent indicators. However, as stated in our previous work (Qian et al., 2015a, 2015b), their non-negligible drawbacks of these fluorophores push scientists to utilize more desirable fluorescent materials to design new biosensors for ALP. Furthermore, most of the preceding fluorescent assays are only capable to qualitatively or semi-quantitatively detect ALP quantity or level, and only several of them (Deng et al., 2015; Jiao et al., 2013; Liu et al., 2014) except those based on organic dyes with high emission efficiency are sensitive enough to accurately determine ALP level in human serum (40–190U/L for adults) (Hausamen et al., 1967). Therefore, the development of accurate detection assays is still in urgent demand.

Since carbon quantum dots (CQDs) possesses many superiorities over conventional dye molecules, noble metal clusters and semiconductor QDs such as stable light emitting, good photostability, easy modulation and excellent biocompatibility, (Baker and Baker, 2010; Lim et al., 2015; Shen et al., 2012) they have been utilized to design and construct different biosensors for alkaline phosphatase (ALP), (Qian et al., 2015a, 2015a; Zhu et al., 2015) acid phosphatase (ACP) (Qian et al., 2015c) and tyrosinase (TYR) (Chai et al., 2015). β-Cyclodextrins (β-CDs), cyclic receptors consisting of seven glucose units linked together, can selectively associate with organic molecules into its hydrophobic cavities of cyclodextrins, which was utilized to design different sensors (Freeman et al., 2009; Haider and Pikramenou, 2005; Pagliari et al., 2004). However, this kind of host-guest recognition is rarely demonstrated in the monitoring of enzyme activity. In the present study, we report a fluorescent biosensor for ALP activity based on β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon quantum dots through host-guest recognition. The β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon quantum dots (β-CD-CQDs) nanoprobe was prepared using 3-aminophenylboronic acid-functionalized carbon quantum dots and soluble hydroxypropyl-β-cyclodextrin, and exhibits bright green fluorescence. Interestingly, the fluorescence of β-CD-CQDs probe will be effectively quenched via photoinduced electron transfer (PET) in the presence of p-nitrophenol due to specific host-guest recognition between β-CD and p-nitrophenol where β-CD moiety in the nanoprobe serves as the receptor for p-nitrophenol. It is found that p-nitrophenyl phosphate ester (PNPP) as the substrate for ALP only exerts a slight influence on the fluorescence of the nanoprobe, however, the presence of ALP can lead to severe fluorescence quenching due to its catalysis of PNPP to p-nitrophenol. Therefore, a convenient, reliable fluorescent assay for ALP can be established in terms of this correlation between quenching efficiency and ALP level or activity. This fluorescent assay possesses enough high sensitivity for ALP level detection in practical sample, and is capable to monitor ALP level in a continuous and real-time way.

Triple-distilled water was used throughout the experimental process. Activated carbon, 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA), 4-nitrophenyl phosphate disodium salt hexahydrate (NPP), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane, hydroxypropyl-β-cyclodextrin, MgCl2 and CaCl2 were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The alkaline phosphatase (ALP, EC 3.1.3.1, 10 kU) from bovine intestinal mucosa, bovine serum albumin (BSA), bovine α-thrombin (thrombin, EC 3.4.21.5), immune globulin G (IgG), acetylcholinesterase (AChE, EC 3.1.1.7) and deoxyribonuclease I (DNase I, EC 3.1.21.1) were bought from Sigma-Aldrich (U.S.A.). Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane buffer solution (Tris–HCl, pH 8.0) was prepared by mixing stock solutions of Tris and HCl. All reagents were of analytical grade and without any further purification.

Activated carbon (2.0g) was put into a mixed solution of sulfuric acid (180mL) and nitric acid (60mL). Then, the mixture was heated at 80°C for 5h. After mixture was cooled to room temperature, it was diluted with deionized water (2000mL). The acquired blank CQDs solution was neutralized with sodium hydroxide. For purifying CQDs, the resulting mixture was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1000Da) for 3 days. The solution inside the dialysis bag was concentrated and dried to obtain the black CQDs powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon quantum dots (β-CD-CQDs) probe
ce:section.ce:para: In order to synthesize β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon quantum dots, the CQDs powder (0.05g) was dissolved in deionized water (30mL), and then the pH of the solution was adjusted to 4.0. A certain amount of EDC·HCl (0.2g) and NHS (0.2g) were added into the CQDs solution. The mixture was stirred for 30min to active the carboxy groups on the surface of CQDs. Then, p-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) (0.25g) was put into the solution. The mixed solution was heated at 100°C for 12h to form an amide bond between APBA and the activated CQDs. The pH of the resulting solution was adjusted from 4.0 to 9.0 with 0.5M NaOH solution, and then hydroxypropyl-β-cyclodextrin (0.35g) was added to the resulting solution. The mixed solution was continued to heat at 100°C for 5h. Finally, as-prepared solution was purified with dialysis bag (1000Da) to remove excessive β-CD, APBA, EDC and NHS. After this step, the β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon quantum dots probe (β-CD-CQDs) was obtained and used for the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of β-CD-CQDs by NP
ce:section.ce:para: For optimization of incubation time for fluorescence quenching by NP, 45µL of NP solution (2.0mM) was added into 3.0mL of β-CD-CQDs (0.133mg/mL) buffer solution (Tris–HCl, pH 8.0). Then, the fluorescence of mixed solution was recorded after different incubation time of 1, 3, 5, 7 and 9min respectively. Under the optimum incubation time, the solution (3.0mL) containing β-CD-CQDs (0.133mg/mL) and various amounts of NP (0.0–132.0µM) in Tris–HCl (10mM, pH 8.0) solution was monitored by fluorescence spectroscopy at the optimal excitation wavelength. After a certain amount of NP is added every time, it is necessary to shake for five minutes. The emission spectra of solutions with different concentrations of NP were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 357nm. All experiments were conducted at 37°C.

The ALP activity was first assessed as extending incubation time with a certain amount of ALP level. The sensing system containing β-CD-CQDs (Tris–HCl, pH 8.0) solution, Mg2+(0.1µM), NPP (70.0µM) and ALP (400.0U/L) was monitored by fluorescence spectroscopy every minute. The fluorescence spectra of the system were recorded from 0 to 40min after the addition of ALP at the excitation wavelength of 357nm. Under the optimal incubation time (15min), the activity of the ALP assay was evaluated. The solution (3.0mL) which contained β-CD-CQDs (0.133mg/mL), 70.0µM NPP and Mg2+(0.1µM) was placed in a cuvette. Then, fluorescence quenching of β-CD-CQDs (Tris–HCl, pH 8.0) was assessed with continuous addition of ALP, where the activity of ALP ranged from 10.0 to 200.0U/L. After adding a certain amount of ALP every time, 15min of incubation time is allowed. The emission spectra of solutions with different amounts of ALP were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 357nm. The temperature of this experiment was maintained at 37°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity test of the ALP assay
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions, the selectivity of the assay toward ALP was conducted as follows. Six possible interferents including bovine serum albumin (BSA), DNA, tyrosinase (TYR), lysozyme (LYS), glucose oxidase (GOx), glucose and acetylcholinesterase (AChE) were selected to evaluate the selectivity of the sensing system toward ALP. β-CD-CQDs in Tris–HCl buffer solution (3.0mL) containing 70.0µM NPP and Mg2+ (0.1µM) was first added into the cuvette, and its fluorescence was regarded as the control. Then, each of the following species including BSA, DNA, LYS, GOx, TYR, AChE, glucose and ALP was separately incubated with β-CD-CQDs solution (3.0mL), where the levels of GOx, TYR, ALP, LYS and AChE are 400.0U/L, and the concentrations of BSA, DNA and glucose are 0.02g/L, 0.3µM and 10.0mM respectively. All the resulting mixtures, which were incubated for 30min, were monitored using fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The morphologies of all samples were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), which was performed on a JEOL-2100F instrument with an accelerating voltage of 200kV. Samples were prepared by dropping aqueous suspensions of the separated fractions of samples onto Cu TEM grids coated with a holey amorphous carbon film. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analyses were conducted using a Kratos Axis ULTRA X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The UV–vis spectra were recorded on a PerkinElmer Lambda 950 spectrometer. The photoluminescence spectra were conducted on a PerkinElmer LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer, and lifetimes were determined using a FLS 980 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of ALP detection based on β-CD-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots from chemical cutting of activated carbon were used as the starting fluorophore because of their good aqueous solubility, strong fluorescence and excellent biocompatibility according to our previous papers (Qian et al., 2015a, 2015b). Carbon quantum dots and 3-aminophenylboronic acid were used to prepare 3-aminophenylboronic acid-functionalized carbon quantum dots (APBA-CQDs), which enables the product to link with hydroxypropyl-β-cyclodextrin through B-O bonds. Hydroxypropyl-β-cyclodextrin was chosen as the receptor for p-nitrophenol instead of β-cyclodextrin because of its higher aqueous solubility. In alkaline solution, APBA-CQDs readily react with hydroxypropyl-β-cyclodextrin to form the final nanoprobe, hydroxypropyl-β-cyclodextrin-functionalized CQDs (β-CD-CQDs). Scheme 1 shows schematic illustration of detection strategy for ALP activity based on β-CD-CQDs. p-Nitrophenyl phosphate disodium salt (NPP) was selected as the substrate for ALP. Under the catalysis of ALP, NPP can be rapidly hydrolyzed into p-nitrophenol (NP), and then the resultant NP enters the cavity of hydroxypropyl-β-cyclodextrin in the probe due to the hydrophonic nature of NP and right size match between NP and the cavity of β-CD. Photoinduced electron transfer between NP and CQDs moiety in the probe then takes place, where NP and CQDs serve as the electron acceptor and electron donor respectively, and then the resultant fluorescence quenching is followed. The fluorescence quenching efficiency is correlated to the amount of NP and ALP level, which can be used to establish quantitative detection method for ALP activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots used for the synthesis of the following nanoprobe were prepared according to our previous papers (Qian et al., 2015a, 2015b). TEM image in Fig. S1 showed that they possess a relatively narrow size distribution ranging from 1 to 4nm, which is consistent with those in diameter in our previous papers (Qian et al., 2015a, 2015b). They can fluoresce bright yellowish green light under UV lamp. Carboxylic acid group is rich on the surface of CQDs due to chemical oxidation approach, which endows the feasibility to covalently link to 3-aminophenyl boronic acid through –CO-NH- bond. EDC/NHS was used to synthesize 3-amniophenyl boronic acid-functionalized CQDs (APBA-CQDs), which is followed by the covalent bonding with hydropropyl-β-CD to produce the final nanoprobe hydropropyl-β-CD-functionalized CQDs (β-CD-CQDs). As-prepared β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe was characterized by TEM, XPS, UV–vis, and fluorescence spectroscopy, respectively. TEM images in Fig. 1(A) shows the size distribution of β-CD-CQDs nanoprobes in the range from 2 to 5nm, which is in consistence with that of original CQDs. XPS spectrum in Fig. 1(B) displays the dominant binding energy signals at 284.4eV, 532.0eV, 339.3eV, and 197.3eV, which correspond to C (–C=C–), O (–CO–NH–, –OH), N (–CO–NH–) and B (–B–O–) in the nanoprobe respectively. In comparison with that for XPS spectrum of original CQDs, the signals from N and B atoms were additional for β-CD-CQDs, indicating that 3-amniophenyl boronic acid and hydropropyl-β-CD have been incorporated into CQDs. Comparison in UV–vis spectra between CQDs and β-CD-CQDs shown in Fig. S2 also clearly indicates their distinct absorption behaviors, where no obvious absorption peaks can be recorded for CQDs, but β-CD-CQDs exhibit two apparent absorption peaks at 233nm and 282nm. β-CD-CQDs probe in aqueous solution exhibits a bright bluish green fluorescence centered at 440nm, which is substantially blue-shifted by 86nm compared to that of CQDs (526nm) accompanying the large shift in optimum excitation peak as shown in Fig. 2(A). This blue-shift in fluorescence of β-CD-CQDs is attributed to the covalent introduction of amino group of β-CD to the surface of CQDs. Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves in Fig. 2(B) clearly illustrated their different fluorescence lifetimes, where the lifetime of β-CD-CQDs probe is estimated to be 4.8ns, much longer than that of CQDs (2.3ns). Based on the relative method, the fluorescence quantum yield of β-CD-CQDs probe is calculated to be 5.3% using quinine sulfate as the standard reference. Stability test of this probe in Fig. S3 showed that the probe is stable under irradiation of natural light within eight hours although it may decompose gradually under irradiation of UV light over one hour. Further results proved that the probe can be stored in natural light for months with almost no apparent change in fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoinduced electron transfer process between β-CD-CQDs and p-nitrophenol
ce:section.ce:para: It has been reported that p-nitropheol acting as an electron acceptor can effectively quench anthracene-derived fluorophores through intramolecular photoinduced electron transfer process (Zhou et al., 1992). Since p-nitropheol is capable to enter the cavity of β-CD, it is speculated that p-nitropheol as an electron acceptor could quench the fluorescence of β-CD-CQDs probe through intermolecular photoinduced electron transfer process. Thus, we evaluated the feasibility for fluorescence quenching of the probe by p-nitropheol. Illustrated in Fig. 3(A), a progressive decrease in fluorescence intensity of β-CD-CQDs probe is observed as the concentration of added p-nitropheol in solution is stepwise increased in the range of 0.0–132.0µM, clearly indicating that the fluorescence of β-CD-CQDs probe can efficiently quenched by p-nitropheol. Although a similar fluorescence quenching phenomenon on CdSe/ZnS QDs by p-nitropheol was reported, little exploration on underlying nature was conducted. (Freeman et al., 2009) It can be noticed that an apparent shift in fluorescence takes place as the addition of p-nitropheol, which implies that added p-nitropheol closely interact with β-CD-CQDs probe to affect its optical property. This point supports that the added p-nitropheol has entered the cavity of β-CD in the probe through specific host-guest recognition. High quenching efficiency in the presence of micromolar p-nitropheol cancels out the possibility of mere dynamic quenching mechanism, and suggests a nature of resonance energy transfer (RET) or photoinduced electron transfer (PET). According to the rule for RET that the fluorescence of the donor is largely overlapped with the absorption of the acceptor, we compared the spectra between β-CD-CQDs and p-nitropheol displayed in Fig. S4. Almost no overlap between fluorescence spectrum of β-CD-CQDs and absorption spectrum of p-nitropheol can be identified, and thus it is reasonable to rule out RET mechanism for the quenching. Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of β-CD-CQDs in the absence and presence of p-nitropheol were further analyzed as shown in Fig. 3(B). Distinct decay curves between them and shorter lifetime in the presence of p-nitropheol than in the absence are consistent with the characteristics of electron transfer. Thus, PET process is proposed as the underlying nature for fluorescence quenching, where β-CD-CQDs and p-nitrophenol serves as electron donor and electron acceptor respectively. Moreover, a red-shift in fluorescence of the probe was observed after p-nitrophenol was added. This red-shift in fluorescence can be ascribed to the influence by host-guest interaction between p-nitrophenol and β-CD moiety on optical property of the probe, where the presence of p-nitrophenol inside β-CD cavity caused a slightly red-shift in both absorption and emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time fluorescence assay for ALP activity
ce:section.ce:para: The preceding observation that p-nitrophenol effectively quenches the fluorescence of β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe inspired us to design a fluorescent assay for ALP activity using 4-nitrophenyl phosphate disodium salt (NPP) as the substrate. The design route is as follows: ALP could catalyze the hydrolysis of NPP into p-nitrophenol, and then the resulting p-nitrophenol is capable to quench the fluorescence of the nanoprobe. This correlation between quenched fluorescence and ALP activity in the proposed detection strategy was first assessed as shown in Fig. 4. The fluorescence of β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe was first recorded, and then was monitored after the separate addition of NPP and NP at the same concentration (70.0µM). Only very slight decrease in fluorescence was recorded after the addition of NPP, however, a majority of the fluorescence up to 70% was quenched after the addition of NP with the same amount as NPP, suggesting the feasibility for the designed detection route. Finally, a certain amount of ALP (200.0U/L) was further added into the mixture containing β-CD-CQDs and NPP (70.0µM), and the fluorescence sharply declined to more than 50% of the original value after 30min incubation. Hence this correlation between quenched fluorescence and ALP level can be used to establish a fluorescent assay for ALP activity in a real-time manner.

Condition optimization for quantifying ALP level was first carried out. The incubation time for fluorescence quenching in the presence of ALP and NPP was assessed in Fig. 5(A). In this experiment, 70.0µM for NPP was selected according to quenching efficiency in Fig. 3(A). It is noted that quenching efficiency is sharply increased within the first ten min for three different ALP levels including 100.0, 200.0 and 300.0U/L, and is slightly changed after 15min incubation, implying this period is sufficient to attain equilibrium. Thus, 15min is chosen as the optimum incubation time. PB buffer solution (pH 8.0) was used in the following procedure because ALP has the strongest catalysis capability under basic condition. Under optimum conditions, the fluorescence of the sensing system containing β-CD-CQDs and NPP (70.0µM) was monitored as the addition of different levels of ALP as shown in Fig. 5(B). The fluorescence is gradually decreased as the increase of ALP level in the range from 0.0 to 200.0U/L. Fig. 5(C) clearly demonstrates the decreasing trend as elevating the ALP level that a sharp decrease at the first stage from 0.0 to 120.0U/L is followed by a smooth decline for the range over 120.0U/L. It can be readily identified that a good linear relationship between the fluorescence and ALP level in the range from 3.1U/L to 80.0U/L as displayed in Fig. 5(D). The regression equation can be expressed as y=−5.1x+919.4, where R2 is 0.993. The detection limit estimated from three times standard error in term of this fitting curve is 0.9U/L, which is slightly superior to that based on disaggregation of CQDs in our previous work (1.1 and 1.4U/L) (Qian et al., 2015a, 2015b), and much better than that based on conjugated polymers (10U/L or 100U/L) (Deng et al., 2015; Li et al., 2014; Zheng et al., 2014). In addition, we speculated that the amount of NPP used in the sensing system possibly exerts an impact on analytical performance for ALP detection, and thus further assessed its influence by changing the amount of NPP. A lower concentration of NPP (50.0µM) was used first as shown in Fig. S5. A gradual fluorescence decline was recorded as ALP level is increasing from 0.0 to 140.0U/L, and also a slight fluorescence shift was also observed. However, the overall quenching efficiency is substantially decreased relative to that with a higher concentration (70.0µM) due to its lower concentration of NP produced from corresponding NPP. The detection limit for this situation is estimated as 2.3U/L, and the linear range is from 7.7 to 100.0U/L. A third condition with the concentration of 100.0µM for NPP was further explored. As shown in Fig. S6, a similar decline trend is recorded when ALP level is elevated from 0.0 to 200.0U/L, and the fluorescence shift is in a similar pattern. The detection limit under this condition is calculated as 1.2U/L, and its linear dynamic range also covers broadly up to 100U/L. By comparing three situations in terms of detection limit and dynamic range, the amount of used NPP in the detection lower than 70.0µM causes a slightly inferior sensitivity, and that higher than 70.0µM does not improve its sensitivity; the amount of used NPP in the detection exerts little impact on the linear range for the method.

In addition, we evaluated the selectivity of constructed assay for ALP detection in the presence of other possibly interfering species including glucose oxidase (GOx), bovine serum albumin (BSA), DNA fragment (DNA), acetylcholinesterase (AChE), tyrosinase (TYR), lysozyme (LYS) and glucose (Glu) separately. As shown in Fig. S7, the separate presence of GOx, BSA, DNA, AChE, TYR and LYS results almost no apparent quenching of the fluorescence in comparison with the blank. However, the addition of ALP (200.0U/L) induces a great extent of quenching in fluorescence, and the quenching efficiency is over 50%, indicating good selectivity for ALP response. Because the nanoprobe was constructed by B-O bonds, where the boronic acid can link to hydroxyl groups of any compound in a competitive way, glucose at a high concentration is considered probably a key interferent for ALP detection in serum. Since it is known that the concentration of glucose in serum of healthy human can be up to 10.0mM, ALP detection is assessed in the presence of 10.0mM glucose. The results show that a 5% enhancement of the fluorescence was observed in the presence of single glucose (10.0mM), and the responses of this assay to ALP in the absence and presence of a high concentration of glucose are very close, clearly indicating that this assay possesses a high specificity. This is first time to achieve sensitive evaluation of ALP level in the real-time way using carbon quantum dots-functionalized nanoprobes through host-guest recognition.

In summary, carbon quantum dots and p-nitrophenol serving as a good electron acceptor/donor pair were demonstrated, and further utilized to develop a reliable and highly sensitive ALP assay based on β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon quantum dots (β-CD-CQDs) nanoprobe through specific host-guest recognition and photoinduced electron transfer. β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe were prepared using boronic acid-functionalized CQDs and soluble hydropropyl β-cyclodextrins. The hydrolysis product of the substrate for ALP, p-nitrophenol, can combine with β-CD-CQDs through their specific host-guest interaction, where β-CD and p-nitrophenol serve as the host and guest respectively. An efficient photoinduced electron transfer process is followed by this recognition, which induces the following fluorescence quenching. The correlation between quenched fluorescence and ALP level is used to establish quantitative evaluation of ALP activity in a broad range from 3.4 to 100.0U/L with the detection limit of 0.9U/L. This study demonstrates that carbon quantum dots and p-nitrophenol can function as a good electron donor/acceptor pair, and an efficient PET between can occur to present the change of fluorescence signal. This specific property and host-guest recognition between β-CD and p-nitrophenol are expected to develop more biosensors in practical detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful to the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21405142 and 21275131).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2016.04.047.
